thinking.text:
I realize the approach of using 1fps frames is missing many commands because the transcript scrolls. But the game has 262 moves and I need to capture all of them. Let me think about this differently.

The video is 190 seconds long. At 1fps, I have 190 frames. But each frame only shows a portion of the transcript. If I could extract the full transcript from each frame and combine them, I'd get all commands.

Actually, looking at the earlier output more carefully, I can see the OCR is working but capturing partial commands. The key insight is that in Zork, each command is shown with a '>' prompt before it. The game transcript scrolls, so earlier commands disappear from view.

However, the game also has a score display that shows "Moves: X". The final frame shows 262 moves. So I need to capture all 262 commands.

Looking at the frames from the HQ set (5fps), I have more temporal resolution. Let me try a different approach: instead of using OCR on individual frames, let me try to find frames where the entire transcript is visible with all commands. But the screen is only 1280x720, so only a portion is visible at any time.

Let me check if there's a better way - maybe I can look at specific frames and carefully extract commands from the transcript portions visible at each point in the video. 

Actually, looking at the OCR output I've been getting, I can see the game commands. Let me compile a proper list by examining the video at key moments. The commands I've seen so far (from various frames) include:

n (north)
u (up)
get (get egg / get various items)
d (down)
s (south)
e (east)
open (mailbox, trap door, etc.)
w (west)
get all
move rug
open case
open trap
light lamp
slay troll
g (again)
get bag
se (southeast?)
sw (southwest?)
ulysse (say Ulysse to cyclops)
put all but lamp
case (put items in case)
temple (go to temple)
open solid (open the barrow door)
get sharp (get sharp object)
pray
put solid (put solid object)
ease (ease...?)
ne (northeast?)
push all
get tool
set nut
wrench
pump
boat
put all but lamp,pump
def late boat (deflate boat?)
echo
drop boat
board
launch
get out
dig sand
shovel
drop red (drop red buoy)
open red (open red buoy)
drop shovel
sw
put bar,emeral (put bar and emerald)
wW (w or west?)
get sack
open sack
put sharp, pot, bug (put sharp object, pot, bug?)
drop all but pump
get fork, trunk, bar,emeral
put all
get lamp,clove,screw
save
drop pair
ring bell
read
rub mirror
put torch,screw
eage (cage?)
put heap
lower cage
ceage (cage?)
drop all
get all from cage
open lid
close lid
set switch
get lamp,clove
get huge, torch
get egg,sword, lamp
kill man
get head, jade,cup,egg,golden
wind canary

This is a lot of OCR errors. I need to verify each command by looking at the actual video frames more carefully.

Let me try a completely different approach: use the HQ frames but only process them every 0.5 seconds or so, and carefully extract the commands that are visible in the transcript.

detail.visible_answer:
{
  "analysis": "I need to rethink the approach. Let me examine specific frames from the HQ set (5fps, 951 frames) which provides better temporal resolution. I should check frames at key intervals to see all commands that appear over time. The 1fps set only has 190 frames, but the HQ set has 951 frames covering the same 190 seconds. At 5fps, I can see the typing animation more clearly and better detect when a command is completed.",
  "plan": "Run a quick test on a subset of HQ frames to verify OCR accuracy, then write a proper extraction script that processes frames and extracts commands more accurately.",
  "commands": [
    {
      "keystrokes": "python3 << 'EOF'
import os
import subprocess

# Test with a few frames from HQ set to verify OCR
frames_dir = "/tmp/frames_hq"

# Check frames around 1.5s to 3s (frames 7-15) where first command 'n' is typed
for i in range(7, 20):
    f = f"frame_{i:04d}.png"
    result = subprocess.run(["tesseract", os.path.join(frames_dir, f), "stdout"], capture_output=True, text=True)
    text = result.stdout
    lines = text.split('\n')
    print(f"\n=== {f} ===")
    for line in lines:
        if line.strip().startswith('>'):
            print(f"  {line.strip()}")
EOF
",
      "duration": 10.0
    }
  ],
  "task_complete": false
}
